name: Tiergarten
notes: Opening section after the start area. Series of medium-speed bends through parkland. Sometimes split into Tiergarten 1 and 2 in finer-grained guides but treated as one section here.

name: Hohenrain-Schikane
notes: Tight left-right-left chicane near the start/finish area. Final sequence before T13. Requires precise braking and rhythm. Sometimes listed simply as 'Hohenrain'.

name: Sabine-Schmitz-Kurve
notes: Fast sweeping left-hander immediately after T13. Renamed in 2021 in honour of Sabine Schmitz (1969–2021), the 'Queen of the Nürburgring' and first woman to win the 24H race. Previously unnamed in many guides.

name: Hatzenbach
notes: Flowing series of S-bends through the forest requiring commitment and smooth inputs. Named after the Hatzenbach stream. Some sources further split into Hatzenbach 1 and 2.

name: Hocheichen
notes: Fast right-hander after Hatzenbach. Name means 'high oaks'. Leads onto the run toward Quiddelbacher Höhe.

name: Quiddelbacher Höhe
notes: High-speed crest section with significant elevation change. Blind over the brow. Name refers to the Quiddelbach valley.

name: Flugplatz
notes: Name means 'airfield' — cars get airborne over the crest. Named after an old airfield that formerly occupied the site. One of the most iconic and photographed sections.

name: Kottenborn
notes: Slight left-hander between Flugplatz and Schwedenkreuz, often taken at full speed. Name refers to the Kottenborn locality. Listed as a distinct section on nring.info.

name: Schwedenkreuz
notes: Very fast, slightly downhill left-hander. Name means 'Swedish Cross', referencing a roadside memorial. Historically dangerous; one of the fastest sections on the circuit.

name: Aremberg
notes: Downhill right-hander requiring heavy braking from high speed. Named after the nearby Aremberg castle ruins. Entry is blind over a crest.

name: Fuchsröhre
notes: Name means 'Fox Hollow'. Fast downhill compression through a valley; cars get airborne at the bottom. Continuous commitment section with limited braking opportunity.

name: Adenauer Forst
notes: Technical section of direction changes through Adenau forest. Named after the nearby town. Adenauer Forst 1 (right) and 2 (left) are distinct apexes treated as one section.

name: Metzgesfeld
notes: Medium-speed right-left sequence after Adenauer Forst. Leads toward Kallenhard.

name: Kallenhard
notes: Right-hand section with a crest on exit. Medium speed, requires precise apex placement.

name: Miss-Hit-Miss
notes: Also called Dreifach-Rechts ('triple-right'). Sequence of right-handers between Kallenhard and Wehrseifen. Off-camber and downhill, demanding precision. Listed as a distinct section on nring.info.

name: Wehrseifen
notes: Tight downhill off-camber right-hander into a small valley, followed by a sharp left and uphill climb. One of the most technically demanding sections on the circuit. Named after the Wehrseifen stream.

name: Breidscheid
notes: Section after Wehrseifen approaching the village of Breidscheid. Medium-speed right-hand bend. Listed as a distinct section on nring.info and in VLN/NLS sector maps.

name: Ex-Mühle
notes: Name means 'former mill', referencing an old mill that once stood here. Right-hand corner leading toward the Lauda-Links section and Bergwerk.

name: Lauda-Links
notes: Slight left-hander going downhill after a crest, named after Niki Lauda. Also called ExTal. Between Ex-Mühle and Bergwerk. Not to be confused with Bergwerk where Lauda's 1976 crash occurred.

name: Steilstrecke
notes: Name means 'steep section'. Fast downhill plunge from Ex-Mühle toward Bergwerk. Significant elevation drop. Listed on nring.info and oversteer48.com as a distinct named section.

name: Bergwerk
notes: Name means 'mine works'. Slow right-hander at the bottom of a valley. Historically one of the most dangerous corners; site of Niki Lauda's 1976 accident.

name: Kesselchen
notes: Fast section across an open plateau. Name means 'little cauldron'. Long high-speed commitment across exposed terrain following the climb out of Bergwerk.

name: Klostertal
notes: Name means 'monastery valley'. Fast downhill section through a narrow tree-lined corridor. Limited forward visibility adds to the challenge.

name: Mutkurve
notes: Name means 'courage corner'. Medium-speed right-hander between Klostertal and Karussell.

name: Caracciola-Karussell
notes: The iconic banked hairpin with original 1920s concrete banking. Named after Rudolf Caracciola. One of the most famous and photographed corners in motorsport. Also called simply Karussell.

name: Hohe Acht
notes: Named after the highest Eifel peak at 747m nearby. Right-hander near the circuit's highest point. The road here once passed directly past the summit marker.

name: Hedwigshöhe
notes: Medium-speed section immediately after Hohe Acht on the descent. Named after a local landmark. Also spelled Hedwigshoehe.

name: Wippermann
notes: Series of corners named after a local family. Wippermann 1 and 2 treated as one section. Medium-speed direction changes on the descent toward Eschbach.

name: Eschbach
notes: Right-hand section named after the local Eschbach stream. Transitions between Wippermann and Brünnchen.

name: Brünnchen
notes: Popular spectator area with two corners (Brünnchen 1 and 2). Name means 'little spring/well'. The second apex is particularly demanding. Treated as one section.

name: Eiskurve
notes: Name means 'ice corner' — treacherous in cold or wet conditions due to understeer on the downhill left-hand approach. Between Brünnchen and Pflanzgarten. Listed on nring.info as a distinct section.

name: Pflanzgarten
notes: Two-part fast section (Pflanzgarten I and II) separated by Sprunghügel. Name means 'plant garden'. Cars get airborne. Extremely demanding at speed. One of the most spectacular sections.

name: Stefan Bellof S
notes: Named after Stefan Bellof who set the legendary 6:11.13 lap record in 1983 qualifying. Fast S-bend immediately after Pflanzgarten.

name: Schwalbenschwanz
notes: Name means 'swallowtail'. Right-left-right sequence through the forest. Technical section requiring precise positioning, leading toward Kleines Karussell.

name: Galgenkopf
notes: Name means 'gallows head'. Right-hand corner near the end of the lap, historically referencing a gallows landmark. Leads onto the long Döttinger Höhe straight.

name: Döttinger Höhe
notes: The longest flat-out section on the Nordschleife, over 2km. Cars reach 280–300 km/h. Named after the nearby village of Döttingen. A full-throttle blast back toward the start area.

name: Antoniusbuche
notes: Name references the St. Antonius chapel and a famous beech tree (Buche) that once stood here. Medium-speed left-right combination at the end of Döttinger Höhe. Marks the transition back to the start area.
